title: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.title: The Evolution and Impact of Infectious Diseases
items.content: Historically, infectious diseases were attributed to miasma until germ theory was established by Pasteur and Koch. The 20th century saw significant advances including the identification of etiologic agents, development of antibiotics, and vaccines leading to the eradication of smallpox. Despite these successes, infectious diseases remain a challenge due to emerging pathogens and antimicrobial resistance.
keyPoints: Germ theory established by Pasteur and Koch in the late 19th century
20th-century advances: identification of etiologic agents, antibiotics, vaccines
Eradication of smallpox as a major success
~16% of all malignancies are associated with infectious causes
Emerging diseases like HIV/AIDS, SARS-CoV-2, Ebola, mpox
Antimicrobial resistance in pathogens such as carbapenem-resistant Enterobacteriaceae and Acinetobacter spp.
Need for antimicrobial stewardship
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Chronic Diseases Linked to Infectious Agents
items.content: Infectious agents are implicated in several chronic diseases, highlighting their ongoing impact on health. Notable associations include Helicobacter pylori with peptic ulcer disease and gastric carcinoma, human papillomavirus with cervical cancer, and hepatitis B and C viruses with liver cancer.
keyPoints: Helicobacter pylori linked to peptic ulcer disease and gastric carcinoma
Human papillomavirus associated with cervical cancer
Hepatitis B and C viruses related to liver cancer
~16% of malignancies have an infectious cause
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.title: Understanding Global Epidemiology in Infectious Diseases
items.content: Infectious diseases are a major cause of mortality worldwide, with significant regional variations. Understanding local epidemiology is crucial for developing appropriate differential diagnoses and treatment plans.
keyPoints: Infectious diseases were the second leading cause of death globally, with 9.6 million deaths in 2019.
~17% of all global deaths in 2019 were related to infectious diseases; ~69% in sub-Saharan Africa.
HIV/AIDS prevalence: 16-20% in South Africa, Botswana, Lesotho; >25% in Eswatini.
Drug-resistant tuberculosis is prevalent in former Soviet-bloc countries, India, China, and South Africa.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.title: Impact of Global Travel on Infectious Disease Spread
items.content: The ease of global travel facilitates the rapid spread of infectious diseases across borders. Historical examples illustrate how human movement has contributed to epidemics.
keyPoints: Pandemic influenza, mpox, and SARS-CoV-2 demonstrate the potential for swift global disease dissemination due to travel.

Historical examples: Yersinia pestis via trade routes; smallpox and Salmonella in Native American populations by European explorers; 1918 influenza pandemic spread through military maneuvers; Hajj facilitating worldwide disease spread.
SARS-CoV-2 caused a global recession, stay-at-home orders for over half the world's population, and ~7 million deaths.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · GLOBAL CONSIDERATIONS

items.subtopic: UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY
items.title: Role of Microbiota in Infectious Disease Susceptibility and Management
items.content: The human body hosts approximately 40 trillion bacteria along with numerous viruses, fungi, and archaea. These microorganisms outnumber human cells by about tenfold and are predominantly found in the gastrointestinal tract but also inhabit other areas like the skin, lungs, oral cavity, and nasopharynx. The microbiota plays a crucial role in metabolism and immune system development. Most infections arise from organisms that are part of this normal flora rather than strictly pathogenic ones.
keyPoints: Humans host ~40 trillion bacteria and countless other microorganisms.
Microbes outnumber human cells by ~10 times.
Major reservoirs include the gastrointestinal tract, skin, lungs, oral cavity, and nasopharynx.
Most infections are caused by normal microbiota (e.g., Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus pneumoniae).
Understanding the microbiota is essential for evaluating infectious diseases and guiding empirical therapy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY

items.subtopic: UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY
items.title: Recognizing When to Consider an Infectious Etiology in Patients
items.content: Determining whether a patient has an infectious disease involves evaluating common clinical findings from history, physical examination, and basic laboratory tests. Infections can present variably, ranging from acute life-threatening conditions like meningococcemia to chronic diseases such as H. pylori-associated peptic ulcer disease, or even asymptomatic cases like latent M. tuberculosis infection.
keyPoints: Infections have a wide range of presentations, from acute to chronic and asymptomatic.
Common clinical findings can suggest the presence of an infectious disease.
Evaluation should consider history, physical examination, and basic laboratory tests.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY

items.title: Importance of Exposure History in Infectious Diseases
items.content: A detailed exposure history is crucial for identifying potential infectious agents and guiding empirical treatment, especially when dealing with drug-resistant organisms.
keyPoints: History of infections or exposure to drug-resistant microbes can alter the choice of empirical antibiotics.
Examples include methicillin-resistant S. aureus, vancomycin-resistant Enterococcus species, and multi-drug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Role of Social History in Diagnosing Infectious Diseases
items.content: A comprehensive social history can reveal high-risk behaviors and exposures that are critical for diagnosing infectious diseases.
keyPoints: High-risk behaviors include unsafe sexual practices, intravenous drug use, and certain occupational or hobby-related exposures.
Examples: M. tuberculosis exposure in funeral service workers; Yersinia pestis infection from laboratory work.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Significance of Travel History in Infectious Disease Evaluation
items.content: Travel history, both international and domestic, can significantly broaden the differential diagnosis for infectious diseases.
keyPoints: Recent travel can expose patients to pathogens not common in their local environment.
Examples: Coccidioides immitis exposure in California; Francisella tularensis on Martha's Vineyard.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Immune Status in Patients with Suspected Opportunistic Infections
items.content: When evaluating a patient for opportunistic infections, it is crucial to determine their immune status. This involves identifying potential causes of immunocompromise such as underlying diseases (e.g., malignancy, HIV infection, malnutrition), medications (e.g., chemotherapy, glucocorticoids, monoclonal antibodies), treatment modalities (e.g., total body irradiation, splenectomy), or primary immunodeficiencies. Understanding the specific immune defect helps predict the type of infections the patient is at increased risk for.
keyPoints: Opportunistic infections often affect immunocompromised patients.

Causes of immunocompromise include malignancy, HIV infection, malnutrition, medications like chemotherapy and glucocorticoids, treatment modalities such as total body irradiation and splenectomy, and primary immunodeficiencies.
The type of opportunistic infection risk varies with the specific immune defect.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Reviewing Immunization Records in Immunosuppressed Patients
items.content: For immunocompromised patients, reviewing their immunization records is essential to ensure they are protected against vaccine-preventable diseases. This step helps prevent additional infections that could complicate the patient's health status.
keyPoints: Reviewing immunization records is vital for ensuring protection against vaccine-preventable diseases in immunocompromised patients.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Vital Signs in Infectious Diseases
items.content: Elevations in temperature are crucial for diagnosing infectious diseases. Rectal temperatures provide a more accurate reflection of core body temperature, being higher than oral and axillary measurements. Recognizing fever as ≥38.3°C (≥101°F) is essential, though it's important to note that fever can occur in non-infectious conditions.
keyPoints: Rectal temperatures are 0.4°C higher than oral and 0.8°C higher than axillary.
Average body temperature has decreased by ~0.03°C per decade since the early 1800s to ~36.7°C.
Fever is defined as a core temperature ≥38.3°C (≥101°F).
For every 1°C increase in core temperature, heart rate rises by ~10 beats/min.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Evaluating Lymphatics for Infection Diagnosis
items.content: Lymphadenopathy is a significant indicator of infection. A thorough examination should assess lymph nodes in various regions, noting size, tenderness, consistency, and matting. Palpable epitrochlear nodes are always pathologic. Localized versus generalized lymphadenopathy can help narrow the differential diagnosis.
keyPoints: ~600 lymph nodes throughout the body.
75% of patients with lymphadenopathy have localized findings; 25% have generalized.
Localized lymphadenopathy is most common in the head and neck (55%).
Palpable epitrochlear nodes are always pathologic.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Skin Examination's Role in Identifying Infections
items.content: Many infections present with cutaneous manifestations, making a complete skin exam vital. Specific rashes can significantly narrow the differential diagnosis of an infection. Attention to details like splinter hemorrhages or Osler's nodes may indicate endocarditis or septic emboli.
keyPoints: A complete skin exam includes both front and back.
Specific rashes help narrow the differential diagnosis.
Splinter hemorrhages, Janeway lesions, or Osler's nodes may suggest endocarditis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.title: Role of WBC Count in Diagnosing Infections
items.content: The White Blood Cell (WBC) count is a crucial diagnostic tool for identifying infections, with different types of leukocytes indicating various microbial causes. Polymorphonuclear neutrophils suggest bacterial infection, lymphocytes indicate viral infection, and eosinophils are associated with certain parasites.
keyPoints: Elevations in WBC count often point to infection; however, many viral infections may present with leukopenia.
Bacteria typically increase polymorphonuclear neutrophils (including bands), viruses increase lymphocytes, and certain parasites increase eosinophils.
Table 124-2 lists major infectious causes of eosinophilia.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.title: Utility of Inflammatory Markers in Monitoring Disease
items.content: Inflammatory markers such as ESR and CRP are used to assess inflammation levels. While both are sensitive, they lack specificity. ESR changes slowly and is not useful for frequent measurements; CRP changes rapidly and can be measured daily when needed.
keyPoints: ESR and CRP measure the acute-phase response indirectly and directly, respectively.
An extremely elevated ESR (>100 mm/h) has a 90% predictive value for serious underlying disease.
CRP concentrations change rapidly; daily measurements can be useful in appropriate contexts.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.title: Assessment of Cerebrospinal Fluid for CNS Infections
items.content: CSF analysis is critical for diagnosing meningitis or encephalitis. Key tests include cell counts, Gram's stain and culture, glucose, and protein levels. PCR analysis offers rapid diagnosis with high sensitivity and specificity but does not determine antimicrobial resistance.
keyPoints: CSF Gram's stain requires >10^5 bacteria/mL for reliable positivity; its specificity approaches 100%.
Lymphocytic pleocytosis and low glucose in CSF suggest infections like Listeria, M. tuberculosis, or fungi, or noninfectious disorders such as neoplastic meningitis.
PCR analysis is increasingly used for diagnosing bacterial (e.g., N. meningitidis) and viral (e.g., herpes simplex virus) infections.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.title: Importance of Pathogen-Specific Testing in Rapid Diagnosis
items.content: Pathogen-specific tests such as serology, antigen testing, and PCR are crucial for rapid diagnosis, enhancing patient care by allowing timely intervention. These tests can identify organisms that are not easily cultivable, which may complicate the diagnostic process but also provide insights into previously unrecognized bacteria.
keyPoints: Pathogen-specific tests include serology, antigen testing, and PCR.
Tests help in identifying non-cultivable organisms with unclear disease relationships.
Rapid turnaround of these tests enhances patient care.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.title: Balancing Empirical Antibiotic Use with Diagnostic Needs
items.content: Empirical antibiotic treatment is often necessary but should be balanced against the need for accurate diagnosis. It's crucial to obtain samples for culture before administering antibiotics, as early treatment can hinder subsequent diagnostic efforts. Once a specific diagnosis is made, antibiotic regimens should be narrowed accordingly.
keyPoints: Obtain relevant samples (e.g., blood, CSF) for culture prior to antibiotic administration.
Empirical antibiotic regimens are broad but should be narrowed once a diagnosis is confirmed.
Adjunctive therapies like IVIG may be used in specific infections despite limited efficacy data.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT
items.title: When to Obtain an Infectious Disease Consult for Improved Patient Outcomes
items.content: Obtaining an infectious disease consult can significantly improve patient outcomes, including reducing mortality and hospital stay duration. This is particularly beneficial in complex cases or when initial treatments fail.
keyPoints: Infectious disease consultation is associated with improved outcomes, shorter length of hospital stay, and decreased costs.
A prospective cohort study showed a 56% reduction in 28-day mortality for patients with S. aureus bacteremia following an infectious disease consult.
Consults are advised when dealing with difficult-to-diagnose infections, treatment failures, complicated medical histories, or exotic diseases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT

items.subtopic: INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT
items.title: Infectious Disease Consult for Complex and Exotic Infections
items.content: An infectious disease consult is crucial when managing patients with complex medical histories or infections that are not typical in the region, as it provides specialized expertise.
keyPoints: Consults should be considered for patients with difficult-to-diagnose presumed infections.
Patients not responding to treatment as expected may benefit from an infectious disease consult.
Specialized assistance is recommended for patients with complicated medical histories or exotic diseases.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT

It is irrelevant to modern medicine.

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a host-specific factor that may predispose to the development of an infection?

items.question: Which of the following infections primarily affect immunocompromised patients?

items.question: Which of the following can cause defects in the immune system?

items.question: What should a physician review to ensure protection against vaccine-preventable diseases?

items.question: Which temperature measurement is considered to most accurately reflect core body temperature?
items.explanation: Rectal temperatures more accurately reflect the core body temperature and are higher than oral and axillary temperatures.

items.question: What is the typical heart rate increase per 1°C rise in core body temperature?
items.explanation: For every 1°C (1.8°F) increase in core temperature, the heart rate typically rises by ~10 beats/min.

items.question: Which lymph nodes are always considered pathologic when palpable?
items.explanation: Palpable epitrochlear nodes are always pathologic.

items.question: What percentage of patients with lymphadenopathy have localized findings?
items.explanation: Of patients presenting with lymphadenopathy, 75% have localized findings.

items.question: Which leukocyte type is typically increased in response to bacterial infections?
items.explanation: Bacteria are associated with an increase in polymorphonuclear neutrophils.

items.question: What is the primary reason for not recommending bacterial antigen tests of CSF as a screening tool?
options: They have low sensitivity compared to Gram's stain.
They require specialized equipment unavailable in most labs.
They take too long to produce results.
items.explanation: Bacterial antigen tests of CSF are no more sensitive than Gram's stain and thus not recommended for screening.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.question: What is a key advantage of using CRP over ESR in monitoring disease progress?
options: CRP levels change more slowly than ESR.
CRP concentrations change rapidly, allowing for frequent monitoring.
CRP is more specific to the type of infection.
items.explanation: CRP concentrations change rapidly and can be measured daily in the appropriate context.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.question: What is a critical step when assessing cerebrospinal fluid for suspected meningitis?
items.explanation: An opening pressure should always be recorded when assessing CSF for suspected meningitis or encephalitis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.question: Which pathogen-specific test can identify organisms that are not easily cultivable and may have unclear relationships to disease?
items.explanation: Universal PCRs can identify organisms that are not easily cultivable and have unclear relationships to disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.question: What is the best practice regarding sample collection before administering antibiotics?
options: Obtain relevant samples for culture prior to antibiotic administration
Only collect samples if the patient's condition worsens
items.explanation: It is best to obtain relevant samples (e.g., blood, CSF) for culture prior to administering antibiotics.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.question: What should be done with empirical antibiotic regimens once a specific diagnosis is made?
options: Switch to a broader spectrum antibiotic
items.explanation: Empirical antibiotic regimens should be narrowed as appropriate once a specific diagnosis is made.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 2) | TREATMENT

items.subtopic: INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT
items.statement: Infectious disease consultations have been shown to decrease the length of hospital stay.
items.explanation: Multiple studies demonstrate that infectious disease consultation is associated with a shorter length of hospital stay.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE
items.assertion: Infectious diseases continue to represent a formidable challenge for patients and physicians.
items.reason: Emerging and reemerging infectious diseases, along with antimicrobial resistance, pose significant threats to global health.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why infectious diseases remain a formidable challenge.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease | HISTORIC PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY
items.assertion: Microbial colonization in the skin and lungs may be highly relevant to disease susceptibility.
items.reason: There is increasing interest in these sites for their potential relevance to host biology and disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · UNDERSTANDING THE MICROBIOTA | WHEN TO CONSIDER AN INFECTIOUS ETIOLOGY

items.assertion: A history of infections with drug-resistant organisms can alter the choice of empirical antibiotics.
items.reason: Knowing whether a patient has been infected by drug-resistant microbes helps in determining possible etiologic agents.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Travel history is only important for international travel.
items.reason: Domestic travel may expose patients to pathogens not normally found in their local environment.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because both domestic and international travel histories are important; the reason is true.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: The average body temperature has dropped over the decades.
items.reason: The idea of a 'normal' body temperature does not consider that temperatures tend to be higher later in the day, in women, and in younger people.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.assertion: Knowing what lines, tubes, and drains are in place helps ascertain potential infection sites.
items.reason: Hospitalization often leads to breaches of epithelial barriers due to medical devices.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

items.assertion: Inflammatory markers like ESR and CRP are specific indicators for diagnosing infections.
items.reason: These markers can be used to assess a patient's general level of inflammation and monitor disease progress/resolution.
items.explanation: While these markers indicate inflammation, they are not very specific for diagnosing infections.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.assertion: Cultures of infected tissue or fluid remain the mainstay of infectious disease diagnosis.
items.reason: They allow identification of etiologic agents and determination of antimicrobial susceptibility profiles.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · DIAGNOSTIC TESTING (part 1)

items.subtopic: INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT
items.assertion: Infectious disease consultations are beneficial for patients with complicated medical histories.
items.reason: These patients often have conditions like organ transplants or immunosuppression that complicate infection management.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why infectious disease consultations are beneficial.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with an Infectious Disease · INFECTION CONTROL | WHEN TO OBTAIN AN INFECTIOUS DISEASE CONSULT
